suggested.title: [Video] "Cold Water Boot Camp" — Search and Rescue Society of British Columbia
[Video] "Reach, Throw, Row, Go" water rescue demonstration

supplementary.title: [Video] YouTube: "How to Survive Falling Through Ice"

competence_markers.description: You have boiled water over a fire you built, from water collection through rolling boil, at least once
You can explain why untreated water is dangerous and what organisms it may contain
You can recite the Rule of Three and explain what each interval means
You have entered water wearing full clothes and can float on your back for at least two minutes
You can demonstrate the Reach-Throw-Row-Go rescue sequence and explain why the order matters
You can dress yourself in three layers and explain what each layer does
You can explain why cotton is dangerous when wet and name a better alternative
You can identify the signs of hypothermia in order and describe what to do at each stage

narration_prompts.prompt: Why can't you just drink water from a stream, even if it looks clean?
narration_prompts.detail: What is actually in the water, and what does it do to your body?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe the process of boiling water from start to finish.
narration_prompts.detail: Where did you get the water? How did you build the fire? How did you know it was safe to drink?

narration_prompts.prompt: What surprised you about swimming with clothes on?
narration_prompts.detail: How was it different from what you expected?
Why is "Go" the last option, not the first?
What happens to cotton when it gets wet, and why is wool different? Use what you observed in the cotton vs. wool experiment.

narration_prompts.prompt: If you noticed someone on a hike starting to shiver and stumble, what would you do?
narration_prompts.detail: Walk through your decisions step by step.

cross_connections.description: Following the Reach-Throw-Row-Go sequence instead of jumping in, checking layering before going outside, performing the full extinguishing protocol — these are habit disciplines applied in a survival context. IC1.1 competence makes safety procedures automatic.

The transition from hunter-gatherer to farmer is directly connected — the student who has boiled water over a fire understands what early humans did every day to survive. Clean water was not a given; it was an achievement.

Boiling water is applied biology and chemistry — you are using heat to destroy organisms. The cotton vs. wool experiment is a materials science investigation. The layering system is applied thermodynamics. Fire behavior is chemistry and physics.

The water survival skills in this packet extend swimming competence into survival competence. Floating in clothes, the HELP position, and rescue awareness are the bridge between recreational swimming and real-world water safety. Fieldwork requires physical competence.

The survival scenario walkthrough is a decision-making exercise under imagined pressure. The more you practice thinking through emergencies calmly, the calmer you will be in real ones. This is the same principle behind IC3.4 (Controlled Stress Exposure).

This packet builds directly on Packet A1. Water purification depends on fire skill. Hypothermia recognition connects to shelter knowledge. The packets work as a system — which is exactly how survival works.

content.why_this_matters:
You already know how to build a fire and a shelter. Those two skills can keep you alive. But they are not enough by themselves — because the other thing your body cannot survive without is water.

Here is the strange thing about water: it is everywhere, but most of it will make you sick. A clear mountain stream looks clean. A puddle after a rainstorm looks dirty. But both of them can contain tiny living things — bacteria, parasites, viruses — that are too small to see and that can make you violently ill. In a survival situation, getting sick from bad water is one of the fastest ways to go from uncomfortable to dying, because vomiting and diarrhea cause you to lose water faster than you can replace it. The sickness makes the problem worse.

You are going to learn how to make water safe — and the first method you will use is one you already know. You can boil it over a fire you build yourself. That single skill — fire plus water — solves one of the most fundamental problems in human survival.

But water is not just something you drink. It is also something that can kill you from the outside. Falling into cold water. Getting caught in rain without the right clothing. Being near a river or lake when someone else falls in. You need to know how water behaves as a threat, how your body loses heat when it gets wet, and what to do when someone is drowning — because what most people would do instinctively (jump in after them) is exactly the wrong answer.

And you need to understand clothing — not as fashion, not as comfort, but as survival equipment. What you wear determines how fast your body loses heat. The wrong clothes in the wrong conditions can be the difference between being uncomfortable and being in danger.
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Water and Survival — The Rule of Three

You learned the Rule of Three in Packet A1 when we talked about shelter:

- **3 minutes** without air
- **3 hours** without shelter (in harsh conditions)
- **3 days** without water
- **3 weeks** without food

Three days without water. That is not three days of being thirsty. That is three days until your body begins to shut down. Your blood thickens. Your kidneys stop working. Your brain stops thinking clearly — and that last part is the most dangerous, because long before your body gives out, your mind gives out. You start making bad decisions. You stop caring about things that matter. Dehydration steals your judgment before it steals your life.

This is why water is the second survival priority after shelter. And this is why the ability to make water safe to drink is one of the most important skills you will ever learn.

### Part 2: Why You Cannot Just Drink Any Water

Water that looks perfectly clean can be full of organisms that will make you seriously ill:

- **Bacteria** — like E. coli and Salmonella. These cause severe vomiting and diarrhea, which make you lose water even faster than you already are.
- **Parasites** — like Giardia. You can get this from drinking untreated stream water. It causes weeks of stomach cramps and diarrhea.
- **Viruses** — like norovirus. Extremely contagious and miserable.

These organisms are invisible. You cannot see them. You cannot smell them. You cannot taste them. A stream running over clean rocks at high elevation can carry Giardia from animal waste deposited miles upstream. The water at a campground spigot might be safe — or might not, depending on the system.

**The rule is simple: if you did not purify it yourself, do not trust it.** Tap water from a functioning municipal system is fine. Everything else gets treated.

### Part 3: Boiling — The Universal Purification Method

Boiling is the oldest, most reliable way to make water safe. It kills everything — bacteria, viruses, parasites. No exceptions.

**The standard:** Bring water to a full, rolling boil. The surface should be churning, not just producing a few bubbles. One minute at a rolling boil is sufficient at most elevations. If you are high in the mountains (above 6,500 feet), boil for three minutes.

**How you will do it:**

1. **Collect water.** Any water source — a stream, a pond, a puddle. If you have a choice, moving water is generally better than still water, but you are going to boil it regardless.
2. **Build your fire.** You already know how to do this from Packet A1. Build it with enough fuel to sustain heat for at least 10 minutes.
3. **Use a metal container.** A pot, a metal cup, a tin can with the top removed. The container goes on the fire or over it, supported by rocks or a grate.
4. **Bring to a rolling boil.** Watch it. When the surface is churning vigorously, start your count — one minute.
5. **Let it cool.** Boiled water is safe but it is also scalding. Let it cool before drinking.

That is the entire process. Fire skill plus a container plus water equals survival. You are connecting what you learned in Packet A1 to its most important practical application.

**What if you don't have a metal container?** This becomes a harder problem. You can boil water in a hollowed-out log or a rock depression by heating stones in the fire and dropping them into the water (called hot-rock boiling). This is a Weathering Storms skill. For now, know that it exists and carry a metal container when you go into the field.

### Part 4: Other Ways to Purify Water (Overview)

Boiling is your primary method — but you should know that other methods exist:

- **Water purification tablets** — small tablets (iodine or chlorine dioxide) that you drop into water. They take 30 minutes to 4 hours to work depending on the type. Lightweight, easy to carry. You will use these in Weathering Storms.
- **Water filters** — devices like the Sawyer filter or LifeStraw that physically remove bacteria and parasites as water passes through a tiny membrane. Effective and fast, but they do not remove viruses. You will use these in Weathering Storms.
- **Sunlight (SODIS)** — filling a clear plastic bottle and leaving it in direct sunlight for 6 hours. The UV radiation kills most pathogens. Slow, requires clear weather. A last-resort method.

For Stretching Branches, your method is boiling. Master it. Everything else builds on the confidence of knowing you can always make water safe if you can make fire.

### Part 5: Water as a Threat — Not Just a Resource

Water does not only sustain you. It can kill you. Drowning is one of the leading causes of accidental death for children. You need to understand how water becomes dangerous and what to do about it.

**Cold water shock:** When you fall into cold water unexpectedly, your body reacts with an involuntary gasp. If your head is underwater when that gasp happens, you inhale water. Even if your head is above water, the shock causes rapid breathing that is hard to control. This is why people drown in water they could easily swim in if they were calm — the cold takes away their ability to think and move.

**Clothed water entry:** Swimming in a pool with a swimsuit on feels nothing like being in water with shoes, jeans, and a jacket. Clothes get heavy. They restrict your movement. They trap water against your skin and accelerate heat loss. You need to experience this in a controlled setting so it is not a surprise when it matters.

**Moving water:** A river or stream that looks calm can be powerful enough to knock you off your feet. Water that is knee-deep and moving fast is dangerous for an adult. Never try to cross moving water that is above your knees unless you know what you are doing. In Weathering Storms, you will learn how. In Stretching Branches, the rule is: stay out of moving water you cannot see the bottom of.

### Part 6: What to Do When Someone Is Drowning

Your instinct when you see someone drowning will be to jump in and swim to them. **This is wrong.** An untrained person who swims out to a drowning victim is very likely to become a second victim. A drowning person will grab anything — including you — and push it underwater to keep their own head above the surface. They cannot help it. It is panic, and it is powerful.

Instead, you use the Reach-Throw-Row-Go sequence. This is taught to lifeguards, to the military, to search-and-rescue teams. It works.

1. **REACH** — If the person is close to shore, reach out with something: a stick, a pole, a towel, a belt, a fishing rod. Lie flat on the shore so they cannot pull you in. Let them grab the object and pull them to safety.

2. **THROW** — If they are too far to reach, throw something that floats. A life ring, a sealed water jug, a cooler, a pool noodle, a tied-off rope with a float attached. Anything. Throw past them and pull it back toward them — it is easier for them to grab something being pulled across their path than to catch something thrown right at them.

3. **ROW** — If you have a boat, canoe, kayak, or paddleboard, go out to them in it. Approach carefully — a panicking person can capsize a small boat. Extend the paddle or an oar for them to grab.

4. **GO** — Enter the water as a last resort. Only if you are trained, only with a flotation device, and only if there is no other option. For Stretching Branches students: your job is never to Go. Your job is to Reach, Throw, or Row — and to go get an adult immediately.

**This sequence saves lives.** More importantly, it saves YOUR life while saving theirs. Memorize it. Practice it.

### Part 7: Floating and Surviving in Water

If you ever end up in water unexpectedly, the single most important skill is **floating.** Not swimming. Floating. Swimming burns energy. Floating conserves it.

**Back float:** Roll onto your back, spread your arms and legs slightly, let your body relax. Your lungs are full of air — they are natural flotation devices. Keep your face up, breathe slowly. You can float like this for hours if you stay calm.

**Clothed floating:** This is harder because your clothes are heavy and your shoes want to drag your feet down. But it is possible, and you need to practice it. Kick off your shoes if you can. Trap air in your shirt by keeping the buttons closed and pulling the fabric away from your body. Relax. Float.

**The HELP position (Heat Escape Lessening Posture):** If the water is cold, floating on your back loses heat fast from your chest and groin — areas where blood vessels are close to the surface. Instead, pull your knees to your chest, cross your arms, and curl into a ball. This is the HELP position. It reduces heat loss and buys you time.

You will practice these in a pool or supervised open water. The goal is not to become an expert swimmer — that belongs to 2.1.1. The goal is to know that if you end up in water unexpectedly, you will not panic, because you have been there before in practice.

### Part 8: Clothing as Survival Equipment

Most people think of clothes as comfort or appearance. In survival, clothing is equipment. What you wear determines how fast your body loses heat — and losing heat is how people die outdoors.

**The layering system:**

1. **Base layer** (next to skin) — This layer's job is to move moisture away from your skin. Sweat that sits on your skin makes you cold. Wool and synthetic fabrics (polyester, merino wool) pull moisture away. **Cotton does the opposite — it absorbs moisture and holds it against your skin.**
2. **Insulating layer** (middle) — This layer traps warm air. Fleece, wool sweaters, down jackets. The principle is dead air space — your body heats the air trapped in the fabric, and that warm air acts as insulation.
3. **Shell layer** (outer) — This layer blocks wind and rain. A waterproof or water-resistant jacket. Wind drives heat away from your body faster than still air — a windproof shell stops that.

**"Cotton kills."** This is a saying in the outdoor world, and it is not an exaggeration. Cotton absorbs water — from rain, from sweat, from falling in a stream — and holds it against your body. Wet cotton provides zero insulation. It actually accelerates heat loss because the water conducts heat away from your skin 25 times faster than dry air. A person wearing wet cotton in cold wind is losing heat at a dangerous rate and may not realize it until they are hypothermic.

Wool, by contrast, retains up to 80% of its insulating value when wet. This is not a small difference. It is a survival-critical difference. When you are choosing clothes for outdoor activities, especially in cold or unpredictable weather, the material matters more than the style, the brand, or the color.

### Part 9: Recognizing Cold Danger — In Yourself and Others

Hypothermia means your body is losing heat faster than it can produce it. It does not require freezing temperatures — people become hypothermic in 50°F weather if they are wet and exposed to wind.

**The signs, in order:**
1. **Shivering** — your body's first defense. Shivering generates heat. This is an early warning.
2. **Clumsiness** — difficulty with fine motor tasks. Fumbling with zippers, stumbling on flat ground.
3. **Confusion** — slurred speech, slow responses, poor decisions. The person may insist they are fine when they clearly are not.
4. **Severe shivering that stops** — this is a danger sign. The body has exhausted its ability to generate heat. This is a medical emergency.

**What to do:**
- Get the person out of the wind and rain — into a shelter
- Replace wet clothing with dry clothing
- Warm them gradually — a warm drink (not alcohol, never alcohol), body heat from another person, a fire
- If they are confused or shivering has stopped — this is beyond what you can fix in the field. Get adult help immediately.

You need to recognize these signs not just in other people but in yourself. The problem with hypothermia is that confusion is one of its symptoms — which means as you get colder, you become less capable of recognizing that you are getting colder. This is why the buddy system matters. Watch the people around you.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Water Boiling Over Self-Built Fire

**Meets standard:** The student collects water, builds a fire using Packet A1 skills, places a container securely, and brings the water to a full rolling boil (surface churning, not just a few bubbles). They maintain the boil for at least one minute. The process is self-directed — you did not build the fire or place the container for them.

**Does not meet standard:** The fire goes out before the water boils (fire management skill still developing — loop back to A1 fire practice). The student does not recognize the difference between simmering and a rolling boil. You had to stabilize the container because it was placed unsafely.

**Common mistakes:**
- **Insufficient fire.** Water boiling requires sustained heat, which means more fuel wood than a quick fire demonstration. The student who can light a fire may not yet know how to manage one for 10+ minutes. This is the lesson — fire management for a purpose.
- **Container instability.** A pot balanced on two sticks over a fire will fall. Teach the tripod method (three rocks supporting the container) or a green-wood crossbar between two forked sticks. Show once, then let them solve the engineering.
- **Reaching over the fire.** Young students may try to check the water by leaning over the flames. Establish the rule: you look from the side, never from above.

### 2. Water Contamination Understanding

**Meets standard:** The student can explain in their own words that water may contain organisms too small to see (bacteria, parasites) that cause illness. They understand that clear-looking water is not necessarily safe. They can name boiling as the primary purification method and explain why it works (heat kills the organisms).

**Does not meet standard:** Vague statements like "dirty water is bad." Cannot explain the mechanism. Thinks that if water looks clean, it is safe.

**How to test:** Show them a glass of tap water and a glass of water from a stream or puddle (collected earlier). Ask: "Which one is safe?" If they say the tap water, ask why. If they say both might be unsafe, they are thinking correctly. Then ask: "How would you make the stream water safe?" Looking for "boil it," not "filter it" (filters are Weathering Storms).

### 3. Rule of Three

**Meets standard:** Can recite all four intervals (3 min/air, 3 hr/shelter, 3 day/water, 3 wk/food) and explain the practical implication — shelter before water, water before food, air is immediate.

**Does not meet standard:** Gets the numbers wrong, gets the order wrong, or can recite it but cannot explain what it means for decision-making ("So if I'm lost, I look for shelter before I look for food because...").

### 4. Clothed Water Entry and Floating

**Meets standard:** The student enters water in full clothes without panic. They can float on their back for two minutes with clothes on. They notice and articulate the difference from swimming in a swimsuit — the weight, the drag, the restricted movement.

**Does not meet standard:** The student panics on entry and cannot recover. They cannot float with clothes on for more than 30 seconds. (Note: if the student cannot do this, they may need more baseline swimming practice in 2.1.1 before retesting.)

**Teaching notes:**
- **First entry will be shocking.** The weight of waterlogged clothes is significant. The student may gasp or struggle. This is expected and is the point of the exercise — better to discover this in a pool than in an emergency.
- **Shoes are the biggest factor.** If the student is struggling badly, have them kick off their shoes first. The difference is dramatic. This itself is a survival lesson: if you fall in, kick off your shoes.
- **Do not skip this exercise.** The gap between "I can swim" and "I can survive in water with clothes on" is enormous. Every drowning prevention program emphasizes clothed water competence for exactly this reason.

### 5. Reach-Throw-Row-Go

**Meets standard:** The student defaults to Reach first, then Throw. They do not suggest entering the water as a first response. They can execute both a reach rescue (extending a pole from shore) and a throw rescue (tossing a flotation device within reach of the "victim"). They can explain why Go is the last resort.

**Does not meet standard:** Their instinct is to jump in. They cannot articulate why that is dangerous. They throw poorly or give up after one missed throw.

**Teaching notes:**
- **The instinct to jump in is strong.** Do not shame it — redirect it. "Your heart is in the right place. But here's what happens to people who jump in after a drowning person..." Explain that drowning people grab anything and push it down. An untrained rescuer becomes a second victim in the majority of cases.
- **Practice throwing.** Accuracy matters. Have the student throw a rope bag or sealed jug at a target in the water 10-15 times. Accuracy improves fast with practice.
- **Reach first.** Emphasize that lying flat on the shore and extending something is the safest option. The student should assume a wide, low stance or lie prone so they cannot be pulled in.

### 6. Layering and Clothing Knowledge

**Meets standard:** The student can dress themselves in three layers and name what each layer does (base moves moisture, insulation traps heat, shell blocks wind/rain). They can explain why cotton is dangerous when wet and name wool or synthetic as alternatives. They have done the cotton vs. wool experiment and can describe what they observed.

**Does not meet standard:** They cannot explain why layers matter (versus just wearing a thick coat). They do not understand the cotton problem. They dress for outdoor activities in cotton hoodies and jeans without recognizing the risk.

**Practical note:** You do not need to replace your child's entire wardrobe. The point is awareness. When heading out for a hike or outdoor activity in variable weather, the student should be able to look at what they are wearing and evaluate: "Am I wearing cotton next to my skin? Is it likely to rain? Do I have a shell layer?" The skill is the evaluation, not owning expensive gear.

### 7. Cotton vs. Wool Understanding

**Meets standard:** The student did the experiment, can describe what they felt (cotton colder, wool retained some warmth), and can connect this to a survival context ("If I fell in a stream wearing a cotton shirt, I'd get hypothermic faster than if I was wearing wool").

**Does not meet standard:** They did the experiment but cannot generalize from it. They memorized "cotton kills" but cannot explain the mechanism (cotton absorbs water, holds it against skin, water conducts heat 25x faster than air).

### 8. Hypothermia Recognition

**Meets standard:** The student can name the signs in order (shivering → clumsiness → confusion → shivering stops). They know the appropriate response at each stage (get out of wind/rain, replace wet clothes, warm gradually, get help for severe cases). They can recognize these signs in the roleplay exercise.

**Does not meet standard:** Cannot name the progression. Thinks hypothermia only happens in freezing temperatures. Does not understand that the confused person cannot reliably self-assess.

**Key teaching point:** Emphasize that hypothermia degrades judgment. The person who is getting hypothermic will often resist help — "I'm fine, leave me alone." The student needs to understand that this resistance is itself a symptom, and that watching out for others (the buddy system) is critical because you cannot trust your own assessment when your brain is cold.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Why You Can't Drink Stream Water
**Strong response:** Names specific organisms (bacteria, parasites, Giardia). Explains that clear water can still be contaminated. Describes what happens to the body (vomiting, diarrhea → faster dehydration). Connects to survival stakes.
**Weak response:** "It's dirty." No mechanism, no specifics.

### Prompt 2: Boiling Process
**Strong response:** Narrates from personal experience with specifics — where they got the water, what fire structure they used, how long it took, what a rolling boil looked like. May mention challenges ("The pot kept tipping so I used rocks to stabilize it").
**Weak response:** Generic textbook description that does not reference their actual experience.

### Prompt 3: Swimming with Clothes
**Strong response:** Specific sensory detail. "My shoes felt like weights. My shirt ballooned up. It was way harder to kick." Shows genuine surprise and recalibrated understanding.
**Weak response:** "It was harder." No detail.

### Prompt 4: Reach-Throw-Row-Go
**Strong response:** Explains the full sequence and the reasoning — each step is safer than the next; an untrained person who enters the water often becomes a second victim. References the fact that a drowning person will grab and push you under.
**Weak response:** Can list the steps but cannot explain the logic behind the ordering.

### Prompt 5: Cotton Kills
**Strong response:** Describes the experiment, what they observed (cotton colder on skin, wool warmer), and explains the mechanism — cotton absorbs and holds water, water conducts heat away from the body, wet cotton provides zero insulation. May mention the "25 times faster" figure.
**Weak response:** "Cotton is bad when wet." No mechanism.

### Prompt 6: Hypothermia on a Hike
**Strong response:** Recognizes shivering and stumbling as hypothermia signs. Prioritizes: stop hiking, get out of wind/rain, find or build shelter, replace wet clothing, warm the person gradually. Considers whether to continue to the car or stop and shelter in place. Recognizes the tradeoff.
**Weak response:** "I'd tell them to warm up" without specifics. Or "I'd keep going to the car" without considering that continued exposure might make things worse.
